name: Programming ASAG Transfer Learning and Text Augmentation
population_or_context: Programming-domain automatic short-answer grading using the SPRAG corpus of short student responses containing natural language, keywords, symbols, identifiers, and expressions.
sample_size: The abstract reports experiments with four data sizes; exact split sizes were not extracted from full text.
methods: Three sentence-transformer models were fine-tuned on SPRAG data with five text augmentation techniques: random deletion, synonym replacement, random swap, backtranslation, and NLPAug.
education_context: Short programming-related objective answers authored by students.
response_origin: Real SPRAG student responses plus text-augmented variants derived from existing examples.
rubric_label_space: Binary classification labels, 0 or 1, according to the ScienceDirect abstract/introduction text.
label_source: Original SPRAG labels; augmented variants appear to inherit source labels, but full-text extraction is pending.
generation_model: No generative LLM identified in the abstract; sentence transformers were fine-tuned as scoring models and augmentation used lexical/back-translation/NLPAug transformations.
generation_method: Random deletion, synonym replacement, random swap, backtranslation, and NLPAug transformations of existing short-answer examples.
evaluation_method: The abstract reports accuracy and F1 comparisons across augmented and non-augmented fine-tuning conditions.
scorer_use: Training augmentation for ASAG model development under limited domain-specific data.
real_response_comparator: Likely yes, but exact test split details require full-text access.
sparsity_or_imbalance_motivation: The abstract states that ASAG performance suffers from limited data and lack of available data in many domains.
synthetic_data_research_role: Text augmentation was used to test whether augmented short-answer training examples improve sentence-transformer ASAG performance under limited-data conditions.
reported_metrics: The official abstract reports that combining random swap and synonym replacement improved accuracy by 4.91% and F1-score by 3.36% over the comparison condition.
limitations: Extraction is limited to the official abstract/search-indexed ScienceDirect text; full tables, exact sample sizes, and split details still need full-text verification.
